metadata.title: Network Protocol Attacks: The Infrastructure Layer
metadata.description: A deep dive into attacks targeting the communication protocols (TCP, ARP, DNS) that power the internet.

vulnerabilities.title: ARP Spoofing (Man-in-the-Middle)
vulnerabilities.concept: Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is used to map IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network. It has no verification mechanism. ARP Spoofing occurs when an attacker sends fake ARP messages, linking their MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate computer (like the Gateway).
technical_mechanics: Gratuitous ARP: The attacker constantly broadcasts 'I am 192.168.1.1' to the victim.
The Victim's ARP Cache updates, mapping the Router's IP to the Attacker's MAC address.
Data Flow: Victim -> Attacker -> Router. The attacker can now read, modify, or drop packets.
attack_vectors: Public Wi-Fi: Attackers use tools like Ettercap or Bettercap on open networks to intercept traffic from other users.
Session Hijacking: Stealing unencrypted session cookies flowing through the hijacked stream.
prevention_and_mitigation: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI): A security feature on enterprise switches that rejects invalid ARP packets.
Static ARP Entries: Manually mapping critical IP addresses to MAC addresses (cumbersome but secure).
VPN: Encrypts the tunnel so even if traffic is intercepted, it cannot be read.

vulnerabilities.title: SYN Flood (Denial of Service)
vulnerabilities.concept: This attack exploits the TCP 3-Way Handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK). The attacker sends a massive number of SYN requests but never sends the final ACK. The server leaves these connections 'half-open,' consuming resources until it crashes or stops accepting new users.
technical_mechanics: TCP Handshake: Normal clients send SYN, wait for SYN-ACK, then send ACK.
The Attack: The attacker sends SYN packets with spoofed source IPs.
Resource Exhaustion: The server allocates memory for every SYN it receives and waits for a timeout, filling up the connection table.
attack_vectors: Botnets: Using thousands of infected devices to target a single server IP.
DDOS: Distributed Denial of Service, where the traffic volume overwhelms the pipe, not just the server resources.
prevention_and_mitigation: SYN Cookies: The server does not allocate memory for a connection until the final ACK is received. It encodes the connection data into the sequence number.
Rate Limiting: Configuring the firewall to limit the number of new connections from a single IP.
Load Balancers: Using strong hardware load balancers to absorb the attack traffic.

vulnerabilities.title: DNS Poisoning (Spoofing)
vulnerabilities.concept: Domain Name System (DNS) translates 'google.com' into an IP address. DNS Poisoning involves tricking a DNS resolver into caching a false IP address for a domain, redirecting users to a malicious site.
technical_mechanics: Cache Poisoning: The attacker sends a fake response to a DNS query before the legitimate server responds.
The resolver caches the fake IP (e.g., pointing 'bank.com' to the Attacker's IP).
Users accessing 'bank.com' are silently served the phishing site.
attack_vectors: Phishing: Redirecting users to exact replicas of login pages to steal credentials.
Man-in-the-Middle: Routing traffic through an attacker-controlled server to inject malware.
prevention_and_mitigation: DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions): Adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records to verify their authenticity.
Short TTL: Using short Time-To-Live settings prevents bad data from sticking in the cache for long.
Randomized Source Ports: Makes it harder for attackers to guess the correct transaction ID to inject a fake response.

vulnerabilities.title: SSL Stripping
vulnerabilities.concept: An attack that forces a user's browser to communicate with a server in plain text (HTTP) instead of encrypted (HTTPS). This usually happens during the initial connection upgrade.
technical_mechanics: The Interception: The attacker sits in the middle (via ARP Spoofing).
The Strip: The victim requests 'http://example.com'. The server responds with a redirect to 'https://'.
The attacker intercepts the redirect, establishes the HTTPS link with the server themselves, but keeps the victim on HTTP.
attack_vectors: Downgrade Attacks: Forcing the connection to use older, vulnerable SSL versions.
Credential Theft: Since the victim is typing passwords over HTTP, the attacker can see them in plain text.
prevention_and_mitigation: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security): A header that tells browsers 'Never accept an HTTP connection from this site, ever.'
HSTS Preloading: Hardcoding the HSTS rule into the browser itself (Chrome, Firefox list) so the very first connection is secure.
VPN: Encrypts the traffic before it hits the local network, bypassing the stripping attempt.

vulnerabilities.title: Dictionary & Brute Force Attacks
vulnerabilities.concept: The most basic but effective method against network services like SSH, FTP, and Telnet. It involves systematically guessing credentials to gain unauthorized access.
technical_mechanics: Dictionary Attack: Using a pre-defined list of common passwords (e.g., 'rockyou.txt').
Brute Force: Trying every possible combination of characters (slow but guaranteed to work eventually).
Hybrid: Trying dictionary words with common variations (e.g., 'Password123').
attack_vectors: SSH Scanning: Bots scanning the entire internet for port 22 and trying 'root/123456'.
Default Credentials: Targeting IoT devices that still use factory default passwords.
prevention_and_mitigation: Key-Based Authentication: Disabling password login entirely for SSH and using cryptographic keys.
Fail2Ban: A tool that scans log files and updates firewall rules to ban IPs that show malicious signs (too many password failures).
Account Lockout Policies: Locking the account after 3-5 failed attempts.
